coredata.dc:title: Improved photoluminescence of di, tri, and tetra-aminobenzene molecules hydrogen-bonded by polyethyleneimine and their application for metal ion sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Haowei
Iso, Yoshiki
Isobe, Tetsuhiko
coredata.dc:description: Higher photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is always a required property for fluorescent molecule dyes used as probes in aqueous environment because of non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations. 1,2,4-triaminobenzene (TriAB) dihydrochloride has been reported as a red-emitting fluorescent dye, although its photoluminescence (PL) intensity is very low. Here, a bright orange-emitting fluorophore was prepared by simply mixing TriAB and polyethyleneimine (PEI) in water at pH 7 and applied to metal ion sensing. The PLQY was increased from 3.1 % to 10.0 % by the addition of PEI into an aqueous TriAB solution. Our experiments by exchanging H2O for D2O and changing the pH from 7 to 1 revealed that the role of PEI in enhancing the PL intensity is attributed to the interaction between the amino groups of TriAB and PEI via hydrogen bonding, the prevention of contact between TriAB and water molecules, and thus the suppression of the non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations of H2O. We further demonstrated this role for 1,4-diaminobenzene and 1,2,4,5-tetraaminobenzene.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
1,2,4-Triaminobenzene
1,4-Diaminobenzene
1,2,4,5-Tetraaminobenzene
Polyethyleneimine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of DiAB, TriAB, and TetraAB aqueous solutions
PL enhancement of TriAB aqueous solution by PEI addition
Effect of PEI on PL enhancement at low and neutral pH
Storage stability and photostability of TriAB and TriAB + PEI solutions
Exploration of PL enhancement by PEI into DiAB and TetraAB aqueous solutions
Metal ion sensing of TriAB + PEI aqueous solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographs of TriAB and TriAB + PEI solutions in H2O at pH 7 under white light and 365 nm UV light, (b) their UV–vis absorption spectra, (c) their PL/PLE spectra, and (d) their PL decay curves.
(a) Photographs of TriAB solutions in H2O and D2O at pH 7 under white light and 365 nm UV light, (b) their UV–vis absorption spectra, (c) their PL/PLE spectra, and (d) their PL decay curves.
(a) Photographs of TriAB + PEI solutions in H2O and D2O at pH 7 under white light and 365 nm UV light, (b) their UV–vis spectra, (c) their PL/PLE spectra, and (d) their PL decay curves.

(a) Photographs of TriAB solutions in H2O at pH 1 and pH 7 under white light and 365 nm UV light, (b) Photographs of TriAB + PEI solutions in H2O at pH 1 and pH 7 under white light and 365 nm UV light, (c) PL/PLE spectra of TriAB solutions in H2O at pH 1 and pH 7, (d) PL/PLE spectra of TriAB + PEI solutions in H2O at pH 1 and pH 7.

(a)(b) Change in PL/PLE intensities of TriAB and TriAB + PEI solutions in H2O at pH 7 during storage in the dark. (c)(d) Change in PL intensities of TriAB and TriAB + PEI solutions in H2O at pH 7 with irradiation time. (TriAB: λ ex = 514 nm, λ em = 662 nm; TriAB + PEI: λ ex = 458 nm, λ em = 602 nm)

(a) Photographs under white light and 365 nm UV light and (b) PL/PLE spectra of DiAB and DiAB + PEI solutions in H2O at pH 7 one day after preparation, and (c) Photographs under white light and 365 nm UV light and (d) PL/PLE spectra of TetraAB and TetraAB + PEI solutions in H2O at pH 7 one day after preparation.
(a) Photographs under white light and 365 nm UV light, (b) PLE spectra, (c) PL spectra, and (d) Relative PL intensities for TriAB + PEI aqueous solutions in the presence of different metal ions.
Schematic illustration of preparation of TriAB solutions in H2O and D2O with and without PEI.
Summary of absorption peaks (λ a), excitation peaks (λ ex), emission peaks (λ em), PLQYs, and PL lifetimes (τ) for TriAB and TriAB + PEI in H2O and D2O at pH 7.

head.ce:title: Improved photoluminescence of di, tri, and tetra-aminobenzene molecules hydrogen-bonded by polyethyleneimine and their application for metal ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Keio University, 3-14-1 Hiyoshi, Kohoku-ku, Yokohama, 223-8522, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Keio University, 3-14-1 Hiyoshi, Kohoku-ku, Yokohama, 223-8522, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Higher photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is always a required property for fluorescent molecule dyes used as probes in aqueous environment because of non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations. 1,2,4-triaminobenzene (TriAB) dihydrochloride has been reported as a red-emitting fluorescent dye, although its photoluminescence (PL) intensity is very low. Here, a bright orange-emitting fluorophore was prepared by simply mixing TriAB and polyethyleneimine (PEI) in water at pH 7 and applied to metal ion sensing. The PLQY was increased from 3.1 % to 10.0 % by the addition of PEI into an aqueous TriAB solution. Our experiments by exchanging H2O for D2O and changing the pH from 7 to 1 revealed that the role of PEI in enhancing the PL intensity is attributed to the interaction between the amino groups of TriAB and PEI via hydrogen bonding, the prevention of contact between TriAB and water molecules, and thus the suppression of the non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations of H2O. We further demonstrated this role for 1,4-diaminobenzene and 1,2,4,5-tetraaminobenzene.

The photoluminescence quantum yield of TriAB molecules was significantly enhanced by introducing PEI around TriAB, which prevented the direct contact between water and TriAB molecules, thereby suppressing non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibration.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent labeling technology has brought the fields of chemistry and life sciences to a new era by combining initially non-luminescent or weakly luminescent molecules with fluorescent molecular dyes, resulting in the generation of highly fluorescent products [1]. They have been used widespread across several fields, including diagnostic imaging [2–4], biological research [5–7], chemical and biological sensors [8–12], and more.

These applications impose stringent requirements on the optical and physical properties of fluorescent molecular dyes. Essential properties such as fluorescence quantum yield, stability, and specific selectivity are always important in these dyes, driving researchers to focus on improving them through various synthesis strategies [13]. In addition, fluorescence quenching is one of the major obstacles in the research of fluorescent molecular dyes. Fluorescence intensity is decreased by interaction with their surroundings, especially in water due to non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations, which is the most common environment in biological and chemical sensing applications [14].

The commonly used strategy for overcoming fluorescence quenching and improving targeted properties is encapsulation of florescent dyes in transparent and hydrophilic silica nanoparticles [15–17]. This encapsulation method suffers from low doping efficiency and requires that the dyes have good solubility in water. This limits the application to most fluorophores. Additionally, this approach limits the accessibility of the fluorophores to target molecules, and the inevitable increase in the size of particles would significantly affect the biological application in terms of cellular uptake or distribution [18].

Another widely used strategy for designing fluorescent molecular dyes is chemical modification. By introducing distinct functional groups into the fluorescent backbone, specific properties can be improved. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), a cationic polymer composed of repeating amino groups, is widely used as a carrier to bind fluorescent dyes to non-viral genes for biomedical research because of its positively charged structure, low cost, and ease of modification [19–21]. However, although there are many reports on the applications of PEI [22–24], most tend to focus solely on its ability to deliver drugs to a specific site, while neglecting its potential applications in optics and fluorescence enhancement. A few of works have reported the increase in photoluminescence (PL) intensity or the prevention of fluorescence quenching in water after the addition of PEI [25–29]. However, the dyes used are too complicated, such as large organic molecules [29] or nanoparticles [25,30].

In this work, we focus on a simple fluorescent molecule, 1,2,4-triaminobenzene (TriAB) dihydrochloride, instead of nanoparticles or large molecule dyes. The fluorescence of an aqueous solution of TriAB is very weak, although the PL properties and biological application of TriAB have been reported [31]. Here we report the improvement of PL properties of the aqueous TriAB solution by adding PEI. We investigate PL properties of TriAB and PEI-added TriAB (TriAB + PEI) solutions in H2O and D2O, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . We also demonstrate metal ion sensing using this solution. Furthermore, we investigate the effects of PEI on PL properties of aqueous solutions of 1,4-diaminobenzene (DiAB) and 1,2,4,5-tetraaminobenzene (TetraAB) tetrahydrochloride.

DiAB (>98 %) and TriAB·2HCl (>95 %) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry. TetraAB·4HCl (>95 %), PEI (branched, weight average molecule weight (M w) ∼800, number average molecular weight (M n) ∼600) and PEI (branched, M w ∼25,000, M n ∼10,000) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Copper (II) nitrate (99.9 %) was purchased from FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical. D2O (99.8 at%D) and other nitrate metals (98 %∼99.5 %) were purchased from Kanto Chemical. All reagents were used as received without further purification. Molecular structures of DiAB, TriAB, TetraAB, and PEI (M n ∼600), are given in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of DiAB, TriAB, and TetraAB aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Each benzene derivative powder (4.0 mg), i.e., DiAB (37.0 mmol), TriAB·2HCl (20.4 mmol), or TetraAB·4HCl (14.1 mmol), was dissolved in 40 mL of H2O or D2O at pH 7 under sonication for 5 min. The resulting solution was then divided into 10 mL portions in bottles, with and without PEI (M n ∼600) (2.1 mg, 3.5 mmol), and subjected to magnetic stirring for 3 h. Herein, the PEI was completely dissolved in the solutions. The molar ratios of DiAB, TriAB, and TetraAB to PEI were 2.6, 1.5, and 1.0, respectively. The final obtained solutions were stored in the dark environment and used for photography. The four-fold-diluted solutions were used for characterization.

To investigate the effect of the molecular weight of PEI on PL properties, an aqueous solution (10 mL) of 1.0 mg TriAB and 2.1 mg PEI (M n ∼10,000) instead of 2.1 mg PEI (M n ∼600) was also prepared in the same way.

UV–visible (vis) absorption spectra of the solutions were measured on an UV–vis optical absorption spectrometer (JASCO, V-750). The net absorbance of each solution was obtained by subtracting the solvent absorbance. PL and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of dispersions were acquired with a fluorescence spectrometer (JASCO, FP-6500). Each spectral response was calibrated against an ethylene glycol solution of rhodamine B (5.5 g L−1) and a standard light source (JASCO, ESC-333). The absolute PL quantum yields (PLQYs) of the dispersions were measured on a quantum efficiency measurement system (Otsuka Electronics, QE-2000-311C). PL decay curves of TriAB solutions were acquired with a PL lifetime spectrometer (Hamamatsu Photonics, Quantaurus-Tau C11367), equipped with 470 nm light-emitting-diode excitation source. The PL decay curves were fitted with the following monoexponential equation:
(2)
f
( t )
= A
exp
(
−
t τ
)
where f(t) is the PL intensity at time t, A is the amplitude, and τ is the PL decay time.

Twelve nitrates of Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, and Fe3+ were used to prepare 2.5 mL of 600 μM metal ion aqueous solutions. Subsequently, each solution was added to a solution consisting of TriAB (0.13 mg), PEI (0.26 mg), and ultrapure water (2.5 mL) to prepare measurement samples for PL/PLE spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL enhancement of TriAB aqueous solution by PEI addition
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of PEI changed the color of the TriAB aqueous solution from dark red to light brown under white light, as shown in Fig. 1 a. This color change is consistent with the change in UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1b), i.e., upon the addition of PEI, the absorption peak shifted from 505 nm to 441 nm and the absorbance increased, as shown in Table 1 . This peak is assigned to the π-π* transition between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of TriAB. The blueshift by the addition of PEI can be attributed to the interaction between NH2 groups in TriAB and PEI via hydrogen bonding. The increase in the absorbance corresponding to the π-π* transition by the addition of PEI can be attributed to the increase in the π electron density of TriAB.

The addition of PEI resulted in a significant increase in bright orange fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, as also shown in Fig. 1a. Fig. 1c shows the PL emission and excitation spectra of the TriAB and TriAB + PEI solutions. PL properties are summarized in Table 1. Under an excitation wavelength of 514 nm, the emission peak of the TriAB solution was centered at 662 nm. The addition of PEI resulted in a significant blueshift of both the excitation and emission peaks to 458 nm and 602 nm, respectively. The excitation and emission peaks as well as the absorption peak are assigned to the π-π* transition between HOMO and LUMO; therefore, the blueshift of the excitation peak caused by adding PEI is similar to that of the absorption peak.

The addition of PEI increased PL intensity by a factor of 3.7. The PLQY also improved from 3.1 % to 10.0 % after adding PEI. This corresponds to the increase in PL lifetime from 0.7 ns to 2.1 ns (Fig. 1d). The similar PL enhancement by adding PEI (M n ∼10,000) instead of PEI (M n ∼600) was confirmed, as shown in Fig. S2. The PL enhancement by PEI addition can be explained by the suppression of non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations of H2O due to the prevention of contact between TriAB and H2O by PEI. To confirm this explanation, we performed an experiment by switching from H2O to D2O.

After exchanging H2O for D2O, the fluorescence of the TriAB solution became slightly brighter under 365 nm UV excitation, and the absorption peak was blue-shifted from 505 nm to 441 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 a and b. At the same time, the PL intensity increased by a factor of 7.2, the PLQY increased from 3.1 % to 24.8 %, and the PL lifetime increased from 0.7 ns to 7.0 ns, as shown in Fig. 2c and d and Table 1. The PL enhancement verified the suppression of non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations of H2O by the exchange H2O for D2O [32]. This result supports the above-mentioned explanation of the role of PEI in suppressing non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations of H2O.

After exchanging H2O for D2O, the fluorescence of the TriAB + PEI solution became much brighter under 365 nm UV excitation, and no shift of absorption peak was observed, as shown in Fig. 3 a and b. At the same time, the PL intensity increased by a factor of 2.9, the PLQY increased from 10.0 % to 25.0 %, and the PL lifetime increased from 2.1 ns to 7.0 ns, as shown in Fig. 3c and d and Table 1. The PL enhancement by the exchange H2O for D2O also verified the suppression of non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations of H2O. The increase in concentration of PEI did not significantly affect the enhancement of PL intensity and PLQY, as shown in Fig. S3. This indicates that even though PEI completely covered TriAB molecules, hydrogen bonds between NH2 groups in PEI and TriAB were repeatedly formed and broken, and thus the effect of PEI on suppression of non-radiative relaxation was limited to some extent, resulting in saturation of PL intensity and PLQY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of PEI on PL enhancement at low and neutral pH
ce:section.ce:para: To verify interaction between NH2 groups in TriAB and PEI via hydrogen bonding, we investigate the effect of PEI on PL enhancement at low and neutral pH. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence brightness of the TriAB solutions at pH 1 and pH 7 was dark. In contrast, when changing from pH 7 to pH 1, the fluorescence brightness of the TriAB + PEI solution became dark, as shown in Fig. 4b. Fig. 4c and d shows the PL emission and excitation spectra of TriAB and TriAB + PEI solutions at pH 1 and pH 7, respectively. When the pH was lowered from 7 to 1, the PL intensities of the TriAB and TriAB + PEI solutions decreased by 1/5 and 1/21, respectively. Note that at pH 1, the PL intensity and the PL peak wavelength of TriAB + PEI were almost the same as those of TriAB. By lowering the pH, electron-donating NH2 groups in TriAB and PEI were converted to electron-withdrawing NH3 + groups by protonation. As a result, the decrease in the π electron density of TriAB decreased the PL intensity. Furthermore, for the TriAB + PEI solution, the electrostatic repulsion between NH3 + groups broke the interaction between TriAB and PEI via hydrogen bonding, resulting in the decrease of PL intensity. Accordingly, we can conclude that a significant factor in the enhancement of PL intensity at pH 7 is the interaction between NH2 groups in TriAB and PEI via hydrogen bonding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Storage stability and photostability of TriAB and TriAB + PEI solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Storage stability was evaluated by measuring the change in PL intensity at room temperature during dark storage. As shown in Fig. 5a–b and S4a-b, after 56 days of storage, the PL intensity of the TriAB solution decreased to 67 % of the initial intensity, while that of the TriAB + PEI solution remained 91 %. Thus, the addition of PEI can improve storage stability.

Photostability was also evaluated by measuring the change in PL intensity under the optimum excitation. As shown in Fig. 5 c and d and S4c-d, after 1 h of excitation, the PL intensities of the TriAB and TriAB + PEI solutions decreased to 95 % and 93 %, respectively, and the shape of both excitation and emission spectra did not change. This result confirms the good photostability even after the addition of PEI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of PL enhancement by PEI into DiAB and TetraAB aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm PL enhancement by PEI for other aminobenzene compounds, we investigate the effects of PEI addition on the PL properties of DiAB and TetraAB aqueous solutions. As shown in Fig. 6 a, fluorescence in the DiAB solution was not visible to the naked eye under 365 nm UV excitation, whereas green emission was observed after the addition of PEI. The excitation peaks at 302 nm and 370 nm and the emission peaks at 400 nm and 532 nm were observed for the DiAB and DiAB + PEI solutions, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6b. The addition of PEI resulted in a 23-fold increase in the PL intensity and a significant redshift of the excitation and emission peaks. Similarly, the PL enhancement of TetraAB by PEI addition was observed to the naked eye, as shown in Fig. 6c. The excitation peaks at 382 nm and 387 nm and the emission peaks at 515 nm and 524 nm were observed for the TetraAB and TetraAB + PEI solutions, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6d. The addition of PEI resulted in a 10-fold enhancement of the PL intensity. These PL enhancements by PEI for DiAB and TetraAB are attributed to the interaction between NH2 groups in DiAB or TetraAB and PEI via hydrogen bonding, followed by the suppression of non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations of H2O due to the prevention of contact between DiAB and H2O and between TetraAB and H2O by PEI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing of TriAB + PEI aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated changes in the PL intensities when aqueous solutions of twelve metal ions (2.5 mL, 600 μM) were filled into the 2.5 mL TriAB + PEI solutions. As shown in Fig. 7 a, Fe3+ and Cu2+ added solutions changed from yellow to dark orange under white light and exhibited luminescence quenching under 365 nm UV excitation, while the addition of other metal ions did not cause any color change or luminescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 7b–d, the addition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ to the TriAB+PEI solution drastically decreased the PL intensity under the optimum excitation.

As shown in Fig. S5a, two excitation peaks at 424 nm and 525 nm and two emission peaks at 616 nm and 663 nm were observed for the Cu2+ added TriAB + PEI solution. In contrast, the Cu2+ added TriAB solution exhibited one excitation peak at 513 nm and one emission peak at 661 nm, as shown in Fig. S5b. Accordingly, the appearance of PL peak at 663 nm by adding Cu2+ is attributed to the breakage of hydrogen bonding between TriAB and PEI, followed by the coordination of Cu2+ to NH2 groups of TriAB. In contrast, the presence of PL intensity at 616 nm after adding Cu2+ is attributed to the survived hydrogen bonding between TriAB and PEI even in the presence of Cu2+.

As shown in Fig. S5c, two excitation peaks at 458 nm and 541 nm and one emission peak at 660 nm were observed for the Fe3+ added TriAB + PEI solution. In contrast, the Fe3+ added TriAB solution exhibited one excitation peak at 515 nm and one emission peak at 662 nm, as shown in Fig. S5d. The appearance of PL peak at 660 nm by adding Fe3+ is attributed to the coordination of Fe3+ to NH2 groups of TriAB with and without the breakage of hydrogen bonding between TriAB and PEI.

A bright orange-emitting TriAB + PEI was prepared by simply mixing TriAB and PEI in water at pH 7 at room temperature. Compared to TriAB, the addition of PEI resulted in an increase in PL intensity by a factor of 3.7, with the PLQY improving from 3.1 % to 10.0 % in aqueous environment. The enhancement of PL properties is attributed to the presence of hydrogen bonding between the amino groups of TriAB and PEI, which suppressed non-radiative relaxation via O–H vibrations of H2O. The same fluorescence enhancement phenomenon was also observed in aqueous solutions of DiAB and TetraAB after the addition of PEI, providing further evidence for the above-mentioned role of PEI. In addition, TriAB + PEI exhibited selective and sensitive detection ability toward Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions through the breakage of hydrogen bonding between TriAB and PEI, followed by quenching fluorescence.
Haowei Tan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Investigation. Yoshiki Iso: Supervision. Tetsuhiko Isobe: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2023.120259.
